coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodots calibrated fluorescent probe of QD@amphiphilic polyurethane for ratiometric detection of Hg (II)
coredata.dc:creator: Jia, Kun
Yi, Keyu
Zhang, Weixi
Yan, Penghua
Zhang, Shuai
Liu, Xiaobo
coredata.dc:description: Because of its strong toxicity to the ecosystem and human beings, mercury is regarded as one of the most hazardous heavy metals. Among various fluorescent sensors that have been designed for Hg (II) detection, the probes showing Hg (II) concentration dependent ratiometric fluorescent responses normally exhibited improved analytical performance. Herein, we have synthesized series of amphiphilic polyurethane (PU) to encapsulate hydrophobic fluorescent quantum dots (QD) via the emulsion confinement self-assembly, leading to generation of QD@PU colloids whose red emission can be selectively quenched by Hg (II) among other metal ions. In order to further improve the Hg (II) detection performance of QD@PU probe, the green emitting carbon nanodots (CD) were synthesized and conjugated with QD@PU, resulting to formation of CD-QD@PU colloidal probe showing ratiometric fluorescent response towards Hg (II), which finally contributed to an enhanced sensitivity and wider linear range for Hg (II) detection. More interestingly, both QD@PU and CD-QD@PU doped stretchable PU membranes still hold naked eye discrimination for Hg (II). Basically, the current work revealed a universal protocol to prepare fluorescent probe for Hg (II) detection using PU and low dimensional fluorescent nanoparticles as building blocks, which will open new way for design and fabrication of other advanced sensors and analytical devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence
Mercury detection
Amphiphilic polyurethane
Carbon nanodots
Emulsion confinement self-assembly

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of QD embedded PU nanoparticles (QD@PU)
Fabrication of CD-QD@PU probe and fluorescence detection of Hg (II)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The FTIR spectra of three synthesized PUs (A) and the pH dependent fluorescent intensity of QD@PU colloids prepared by using different PU matrix (B).

The fluorescence emission spectra of optimized aqueous QD@PU colloids under different excitation wavelength (A), in the presence of 50 μM different metal ions (B), different Hg (II) concentration ranging from 1 μM to 10 μM (C) and dose-dependent curve of fluorescence quenching degree versus Hg (II) concentration (D).
The deconvoluted XPS spectra of S2p (A, B) and N1s (C, D) for pristine QD@PU colloids before (A, C) and after incubation with mercury ions (B, D).

The fluorescent emission spectra of CD obtained by using different feeding contents of BA-ph (A, shown inset was the chemical structure of BA-ph and obtained CD colloids solution under UV light), FTIR spectra (B), TEM image (C, inset was size distribution histogram) and high resolution TEM image (D) of optimized CD colloids.
The fluorescence emission spectra of obtained CD in the presence of different metal ions (A), full scale XPS spectra (B), deconvoluted C1s (C) and N1s (D) spectra of prepared CD.

The fluorescence emission spectra of CD-QD@PU conjugates with different compositions (A), fluorescent emission spectra of optimized CD-QD@PU probe in the presence of Hg (II) within lower (B) and higher (C) concentration range, the ratiometric dose-dependent curves of optimized CD-QD@PU probe for Hg (II) detection.

The images of WPU emulsion and fluorophore blended WPU solution (A), stretchable (B) and twisted (C) fluorescent membrane containing different fluorophores, as well as fluorescent membrane for Hg (II) detection (D).
The synthesis route of amphiphilic polyurethane (PU) and carbon nanodots (CD) as well as the construction of dual-emissive CD-QD@PU probe for ratiometric Hg (II) detection.
The comparison of fluorescence detection of Hg (II) by different probes.
The analytical performance of CD-QD@PU probe for Hg (II) detection in real samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodots calibrated fluorescent probe of QD@amphiphilic polyurethane for ratiometric detection of Hg (II)

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials and Energy, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Because of its strong toxicity to the ecosystem and human beings, mercury is regarded as one of the most hazardous heavy metals. Among various fluorescent sensors that have been designed for Hg (II) detection, the probes showing Hg (II) concentration dependent ratiometric fluorescent responses normally exhibited improved analytical performance. Herein, we have synthesized series of amphiphilic polyurethane (PU) to encapsulate hydrophobic fluorescent quantum dots (QD) via the emulsion confinement self-assembly, leading to generation of QD@PU colloids whose red emission can be selectively quenched by Hg (II) among other metal ions. In order to further improve the Hg (II) detection performance of QD@PU probe, the green emitting carbon nanodots (CD) were synthesized and conjugated with QD@PU, resulting to formation of CD-QD@PU colloidal probe showing ratiometric fluorescent response towards Hg (II), which finally contributed to an enhanced sensitivity and wider linear range for Hg (II) detection. More interestingly, both QD@PU and CD-QD@PU doped stretchable PU membranes still hold naked eye discrimination for Hg (II). Basically, the current work revealed a universal protocol to prepare fluorescent probe for Hg (II) detection using PU and low dimensional fluorescent nanoparticles as building blocks, which will open new way for design and fabrication of other advanced sensors and analytical devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury ions have been considered as one of the most hazardous heavy metal ions, mainly because of their strong toxicity to human beings and persistent damage to the soil, water and other resources [1–3]. Specifically, different chemical forms of mercury ions are easily accumulated in human body and cause irreversible damage to central nervous and reproduction system, resulting to the notorious Minamata disease [4]. For this reason, the maximum permitted residual mercury ions in drinking water should be less than 30 nM according to the World Health Organization (WHO) and National Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [5]. Considering their strong toxicity, it is of great importance to develop new materials and technologies for selective and sensitive detection of mercury ions.

Different methods have already been employed for Hg (II) detection. For instance, the atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) as well as electrochemiluminescence (ECL) techniques allow selective detection of Hg (II) in a reasonable sensitivity [6–10]. However, these technologies are limited by either sophisticated instruments/professional personnel or the complicated sample pre-treatments. In this sense, the fluorescence spectroscopy is able to provide sensitive, selective and rapid detection for Hg (II) as long as appropriate fluorescent probe is used [11–15]. Moreover, fluorescent probes can be easily integrated with other systems to enable the so-called point-of-care testing [16–19] or simultaneous adsorption-detection [20–22]. Particularly, the semiconductor quantum dots (QD) have been regarded as promising fluorescent probe for detection of various heavy metal ions, mainly due to their high fluorescence quantum yields, sharp emission wavelength and wide excitation wavelength [23–25]. Meanwhile, the appropriately designed surface ligands and defects of QD have exhibited specific responses to certain heavy metal ions, which in turn renders the QD with specificity in heavy metal ions detection [26]. However, the high quality QD are basically hydrophobic and easily aggregated in aqueous solution to lose their fluorescence emission, thus the phase transfer of hydrophobic QD into aqueous solution without obvious fluorescent quenching is considered as the essential step for their practical analytical applications [27–30]. In this sense, the co-assembly of appropriately designed block copolymers with QD to ensure its aqueous fluorescence has lately received increasing research interests.

On the other hand, the fluorescent intensity variation versus target concentration is widely used for many published works on heavy metal ions detection. But the fluorescent emission intensity of probe could also be alternated by the instrumental fluctuation or artificial operations, which would deteriorate analytical performance, especially for the fluorophores having single emission band. For this reason, the protocols, based on the ratiometric fluorescent signals from probes that exhibit multiple emission bands, normally exhibit improved analytical performance [31–33]. Therefore, the fluorophores exhibiting stable fluorescence emission in the presence of various heavy metal ions could be employed as reliable internal standard to calibrate the instrumental interference. As a kind of promising fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon nanodots (CD) have witnessed increasing development due to their water dispersion, biocompatibility, easy fabrication as well as tunable fluorescent properties [34–37]. In addition, the fluorescent emission wavelength and quantum yield of carbon nanodots can be modulated by introducing heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, boron [38–40]. Thus, the heteroatom doped CD have been intensively used in fluorescence sensing.

In this work, we have synthesized series of amphiphilic polyurethane (PU) and water dispersible N, S co-doped carbon nanodots (CD). Subsequently, the PU has been used as matrix to encapsulate the hydrophobic red-emitting QD via the emulsion self-assembly, leading to the hydrophilic QD@PU probe whose fluorescent emission in aqueous solution can be selectively quenched by Hg (II). On the other hand, since the fluorescence emission of synthesized CD remained stable in the presence of Hg (II), the CD served as internal standard to construct conjugates CD-QD@PU colloids via simple blending. It was found that the optimized CD-QD@PU conjugates exhibited dual emissive bands and ratiometric fluorescence responses towards different concentrations of Hg (II), finally contributing to improved analytical performance in mercury detection when compared to that of single band emitting QD@PU.

Polyethylene glycol (PEG, molecular weight of 1000), polytetramethylene ether glycol (PTMG, molecular weight of 1000), acetone, 1, 4-butanediol (BDO, AR), 2, 2-dihydroxymethylpropionic (DMPA, AR), dibutyltin dilaurate (DBTDL, AR), sulfuric acid (98%) and dichloromethane (DCM, AR) were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co. Ltd. Isophorone disocyanate (IPDI, 99 %) was acquired from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Benzoxazine bisphthalonitrile (BA-ph) was synthesized in our lab (see characterization in Fig. S1 of Supporting information). Hydrophobic core-shell semiconductor quantum dots with fluorescence emission at 600 nm (QD, ZnCdSe/ZnS) were obtained from Wuhan Jiayuan Quantum Dots.

A series of polyurethanes (PU) were synthesized via the addition polymerization between IPDI and different polyols (PEG1000 or PTMG1000) according to the feeding ratios shown in Table S1. In a typical synthesis, the PEG1000 was firstly treated at 120 °C in vacuum for 3 h to remove residue H2O. Next, IPDI (8.89 g, 0.04 mol) was added into a three-necked round-bottom flask equipping with a mechanical stirrer and a condenser, followed by dropwise addition of PEG1000 (20 g, 0.02 mol) and stirred at 80 °C for 3 h under nitrogen protection. Then, the reaction mixture was diluted with 10 mL acetone, followed by addition of DMPA (1.61 g, 0.012 mol), BDO (0.72 g, 0.008 mol) and catalyst DBTDL (0.1 wt%). The polymerization was terminated after stirring at 80 °C for another 3 h, and then the mixture was cooled to ambient temperature. Finally, the mixture was diluted with DCM to have a solid content of 40 wt% and stored in a sealed bottle for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of QD embedded PU nanoparticles (QD@PU)
ce:section.ce:para: QD@PU nanoparticles were prepared via the emulsion confinement self-assembly. Typically, 10 mg PU (PTMG-PU, PEG-PU or PTMG-PEG-PU) were dissolved in 2 mL DCM containing 0.1 mg fluorescent QD (with red emission at 625 nm), followed by injection into 10 mL aqueous solution. Next, the mixtures were emulsified completely under continuously stirring at room temperature and then completely evaporated the DCM to obtain the uniform emulsion of QD@PU colloids. In order to evaluate the fluorescence stability of QD@PU colloids, the fluorescence spectra were recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer from aqueous solution under different dilution ratios and ionic strength.

The green emissive CD were synthesized via a hydrothermal method. The precursor of BA-ph (0.5 mmol) were added into 10 mL of deionized water containing 50 μL of sulfuric acid, and stirred for 30 min. The resultant mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 10 h, followed by cooling to room temperature. Then, the resultant mixture was filtered with a polyethersulfone membrane (0.22 µm) to remove large particles to obtain reddish colloids solution of CD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CD-QD@PU probe and fluorescence detection of Hg (II)
ce:section.ce:para: Hybrid probes were prepared with the combination of CD solution and QD@PU colloids with different volume ratios via a simple blending method. In order to evaluate the analytical performance of the probes for mercury ions, different concentrations of mercury ion aqueous solutions were added to probe colloids and their fluorescence spectra were recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer. Furthermore, the solid probe was prepared in transparent flexible membranes, which were obtained by casting the waterborne PU (WPU) emulsion containing different fluorophores (QD@PU, CD, CD-QD@PU probe) on a Teflon plate and dried in vacuum at 50 °C for 48 h.

The chemical structures of the synthesized samples were verified by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Shimadzu 8400 S, KBr) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab Xi+). Meanwhile, the morphology of the prepared microparticles and beads was observed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, JEM 2100 F). In addition, the fluorescence spectra of the as-prepared PU nanoparticles and CDs were characterized with a F98 spectrofluorometer and microscope coupled laser fluoroscopy. Digital photos of real samples were recorded by a smartphone under visible and 365 nm UV light, respectively.

Previous works have shown that the emulsion confinement co-self-assembly of block copolymer with hydrophobic QD is an effective protocol to fabricate water dispersible micro/nano-particles, which integrate the strong fluorescence of QD and diverse functionalities of polymer matrix [41–47]. As a kind of classical block copolymer, PU have been widely used as the functional matrix for various application [48]. For this reason, we herein have synthesized a range of PU with different chemical structures (see Scheme 1), which have been further employed as matrix to encapsulate red-emitting hydrophobic QD to fabricate water dispersible QD@PU nanoparticles. Moreover, we found that the red-emission from aqueous QD@PU colloids solution can be selectively quenched by Hg (II), leading to the application of QD@PU as fluorescent probe for Hg (II) detection. In order to further improve its analytical performance, the CD with stable green emission was synthesized and introduced into QD@PU colloids solution as reliable internal standard to calibrate the instrumental variations. Finally, the dual-emissive CD-QD@PU conjugates exhibited promising analytical performance for Hg (II) detection in terms of sensitivity, reproducibility as well as repeatability.

In order to select the preferred PU matrix to enable the effective phase transfer of hydrophobic QD into aqueous solution, we have synthesized three PUs containing different soft segments. As shown in Fig. 1A, the absence of isocyanate peak at 2205 cm−1 from the FTIR spectra indicated the complete polymerization of PUs. Meanwhile, all the samples demonstrated the stretching vibration peaks of N-H at 3320 cm−1, CO at 1710 cm−1 and C-O-C at 1110 cm−1, which were attributed to the characteristic features of carbamate [49]. Moreover, different soft segments of PUs could be discriminated via the stretching vibration bands of -CH2 at 2860 cm−1 and 2930 cm−1. Furthermore, it was clear from Fig. 1B that when these PUs were employed as matrix to prepare QD@PU nanoparticles following same protocol, the colloids solution obtained by using PTMG-PEG-PU matrix showed strongest fluorescent emission than those of other two samples within pH range from 3 to 13. Meanwhile, it was clear from the TEM images in Fig. S2 that the QD were uniformly dispersed within PU matrix without any noticeable aggregation. Therefore, the amphiphilic PTMG-PEG-PU is regarded as the optimized matrix for enabling phase transfer of hydrophobic QD into aqueous solution, and was used as the optimized matrix in preparation of QD@PU samples for following experiments.

Next, the fluorescent properties of optimized QD@PU colloids were tested. As shown in Fig. 2A, the colloids exhibited typical QD fluorescent emission at 621 nm, the emission intensity was firstly increased and then decreased with the increasing of excitation wavelength from 345 nm to 385 nm, and the highest emission intensity was recorded when excited at 365 nm. In addition, the fluorescent emission of QD@PU colloids was nearly independent of ionic strength (see Fig. S3A), and the emission profile exhibited unchanged wavelength and gradually declined intensity along with dilution according to Fig. S3B. More interestingly, it was clear from Fig. 2B that the red emission of QD@PU can be selectively quenched by Hg (II) among other seven metal ions at same concentration of 50 μM. Furthermore, the fluorescent response of QD@PU colloids towards Hg (II) with different concentrations were recorded. According to Fig. 2C, the typical red emission was gradually quenched along with increasing of Hg (II) concentration from 1 μM to 10 μM. Moreover, the red-emission quenching degree of QD@PU exhibited somewhat linear relationship versus corresponding Hg (II) concentration (see Fig. 2D). However, the lower correlation coefficient (0.94) obtained from linear fitting implied that the analytical performance of QD@PU colloids for Hg (II) detection still required further optimization.

The XPS technique was employed for probe the sensing mechanism of QD@PU towards Hg (II). As show in Fig. S4A, the feature peaks corresponding to O1s, N1s, C1s and S2p were all detected from both samples, while the Hg 4 f band only appeared in the full scale XPS spectrum of QD@PU colloids after incubation with Hg (II). Meanwhile, both samples demonstrated identical deconvoluted C1s (Fig. S4B, C) and O1s (Fig. S4D, E) spectra, which proved that the carboxyl groups of QD@PU colloids were not involved in the responses to Hg (II). However, the obvious alternation of sulfur and nitrogen chemical states were detected after the incubation of Hg (II) with QD@PU. For instance, the S2p peak at 161.78 eV corresponding to Zn-S of QD@PU ( Fig. 3A) was shifted to 164.08 eV after exposure to Hg (II), the latter peak was ascribed to S2p of Hg-S bonding as shown in Fig. 3B. In addition, the deconvoluted N1s spectra also displayed similar variation, as the C-NH-C peak at 404.78 eV from QD@PU (Fig. 3C) was declined and shifted to 405.18 eV after incubation with Hg (II) according to Fig. 3D. All these results prove that the Hg (II) is diffused inside QD@PU colloids and partially replaces the surface zinc defects of QD, which is due to the enhanced sulphophile affinity of Hg (II) versus Zn [24]. Meanwhile, the carbamate of PU shell might also contribute to the affinity of QD@PU probe towards Hg (II).

Although the QD@PU colloids exhibited specific fluorescent quenching response towards Hg (II), the analytical performance including linearity and sensitivity were still limited. Therefore, we have synthesized the green emitting CD (see details in experimental section). It was clear from Fig. 4A that the CD basically exhibited green emission around 500 nm and its emission profile was highly dependent on the feeding concentration of BA-ph precursor. Specifically, the similar emission spectrum with higher peak intensity was detected as the BA-ph content increased from 0.25 mM to 0.5 mM, while blue-shifted emission spectrum bearing double emission bands was observed from CD colloids prepared by using highest concentration (1 mM) of BA-ph. This could be due to the intermolecular crosslinking of high concentration BA-ph compound at high temperature (see DSC curve in Fig. S5), leading to formation of oligomers that could also serve as additional precursors for CD formulation. Considering the single emission profile and strongest intensity, the CD prepared from 0.5 mM BA-ph precursor was selected as optimized sample for the following experiments. It should be noted that the fluorescence emission of the optimized CD colloids was steadily decreased along with dilution and its maximum emission wavelength was nearly independent of excitation wavelength as shown in Fig. S6. Moreover, Fig. 4B demonstrated that the absorption bands corresponding to stretching vibrations of amino N-H (3134 cm−1), CN (1506 cm−1), C-N-C (1404 cm−1) and -SO3- (1072 cm−1) were all detected from FTIR spectrum of CD, indicating the N, S co-doping feature of prepared CD. In addition, the average size of prepared CD was measured as 50 nm according to the TEM image in Fig. 4C. Lastly, a lattice spacing of 0.318 nm was observed from the high resolution TEM image in Fig. 4D, which was attributed to the typical crystal feature of CD.

Prior to its sensing application, the fluorescent emission and chemical states of obtained CD were characterized. It was clear from Fig. 5A that the green emission of CD remained unchanged in the presence of eight tested metal ions. Meanwhile, the O1s, C1s, N1s and S2p bands were all detected from the full scale XPS spectrum of prepared CD according to Fig. 5B and Fig. S7. More specifically, Fig. 5C showed that the typical π-π * , CN, C-O band were respectively detected at 293.18 eV, 295.98 eV, 288.98 eV and 286.58 eV from the deconvoluted C1s XPS spectrum [50]. In addition, the chemical states of nitrogen were probed in Fig. 5D, where the peaks appeared at 401.98 eV, 401.38 eV and 399.58 eV were ascribed to the graphitic N, N-H and amino N, respectively.

Due to its metal ions independent stable fluorescence emission and water dispersion, the prepared CD could server as reliable internal standard to enhance the analytical performance of QD@PU towards Hg (II). Firstly, we have tested the fluorescent emission evolution of CD-QD@PU conjugates with different compositions. It was clear from Fig. 6A that the red-emission of QD@PU was obviously enhanced after introducing of CD due to energy transfer (see spectroscopy analysis in Fig. S8). Although the green emission intensity of conjugates was steadily increased as rising of CD content, the intended red emission peak showed highest intensity when equal volume of CD and QD@PU colloids were used, and this sample was thus selected as optimized one to construct following ratiometric fluorescence probe. Next, the fluorescence emission spectra of optimized CD-QD@PU conjugate in the presence of lower concentrations of Hg (II) and higher contents of Hg (II) were recorded in Fig. 6B and Fig. 6C, respectively. No matter in which concentration range, the green emission band of CD-QD@PU probe remained unchanged, while the red emission peak intensity of CD-QD@PU probe was gradually quenched by the increasing content of Hg (II). Lastly, when the ratiometric fluorescence quenching degree (defined as ∆I621/∆I490, where ∆I621 and ∆I490 was Hg (II) induced fluorescent quenching degree of emission intensity at 621 nm and 490 nm, respectively.) was plotted against Hg (II) concentration, a good linearity correlation with coefficient approaching to 0.99 was obtained in the wide Hg (II) content range from 0.5 μM to 10 μM as shown in Fig. 6D. The limit of detection was calculated as 10.5 nM according to the 3σ rule (signal-to-noise ratio of 3). The CD-QD@PU probe also exhibited good reproducibility for Hg (II) detection according to the results shown in Fig. S9. Finally, the analytical performance of present probe versus recently published fluorescent probe was compared in Table 1. It should be noted that the current CD-QD@PU colloids exhibited competitive sensitivity and wider linear range than those of previously published works [51–56].

Furthermore, the practical sensing performance of optimized probe for Hg (II) detection was evaluated. Specifically, known concentration of Hg (II) was respectively added into tap water and lake water, after recording their fluorescent quenching degrees to CD-QD@PU probe, the Hg (II) concentration was calculated based on the dose-dependent curve in Fig. 6D. It was clear from Table 2 that the calculated Hg (II) concentration from both mercury spiked tap water and lake water were close to their corresponding concentration verified by ICP technique. Meanwhile, the recovery rate and relative standard derivation (RSD) was calculated as 92.98%− 106.71% and 0.39%− 1.65%, respectively, all these results illustrated that the CD-QD@PU probe allowed detection of Hg (II) presented in real environmental samples.

Finally, we also evaluated the fluorescence responses of CD-QD@PU probe in solid state. In this proof-of-concept application, the CD-QD@PU colloids were blended with waterborne polyurethane (WPU) emulsion to fabricate stretchable fluorescent membrane. It was found from Fig. 7A that all the fluorescence emission of CD, QD@PU and CD-QD@PU colloids can be well preserved when they were blended with WPU emulsion or incorporated in WPU membrane. Furthermore, their fluorescence emission still can be easily detected when the fluorophores doped WPU membrane were stretched (Fig. 7B) or twisted (Fig. 7C). Especially, the red fluorescence emission of composites membranes firstly increased and then decreased along with the increasing of stretching ratios according to Fig. S10. More interestingly, both the QD@PU and CD-QD@PU probe doped WPU membrane also exhibited naked-eye detectable fluorescence variation upon Hg (II) exposure according to Fig. 7D.

In this work, the amphiphilic polyurethane was synthesized and employed as matrix to enable the phase transfer of hydrophobic red emission fluorescent QD into aqueous solution, leading to QD@PU colloidal probe for selective detection of Hg(II). In addition, the green emitting carbon nanodots (CD) were synthesized via the hydrothermal reaction of a benzoxazine phthalonitrile monomer in concentrated H2SO4. Due to its stable fluorescence emission, the synthesized CD was blended with QD@PU to obtain the CD-QD@PU conjugates that exhibited ratiometric fluorescent responses and improved analytical performance for Hg (II) detection. More interestingly, the fluorescence of both QD@PU and CD-QD@PU colloids was well preserved after being incorporated into transparent PU membrane, which paved the way for fabrication of versatile flexible fluorescent sensor for Hg (II) detection in the future.

Kun Jia: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Keyu Yi: Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Weixi Zhang: Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation. Penghua Yan: Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources. Shuai Zhang: Formal analysis, Investigation. Xiaobo Liu: Project administration, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully thank the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (52173068), Sichuan Science and Technology Program (2021YFH0023, 2022YFS0006), Guizhou Science and Technology Program (2020-4Y189) the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (ZYGX2019J026) and Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2022A1515010821).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132443.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrochim. Acta Part A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Kun Jia obtained Ph.D. degree in materials science from University of Technology of Troyes in France in 2013. Currently, Dr. Kun Jia is the professor in chemistry at University of Electronic Science and Technology of China (UESTC). His research interests lie in the field of optical bio/chemical sensors based on flexible functional polymeric substrates and inorganic photonic nanoparticles.

Keyu Yi is currently a master student under supervision of Prof. Kun Jia at School of Materials and Energy from UESTC. His research focuses on the polyurethane synthesis and construction of flexible sensing devices.

Weixi Zhang obtained Ph.D. degree in organic chemistry from Sichuan University. Dr. Zhang is currently a postdoctoral fellow in the group of Prof. Xiaobo Liu at UESTC. His main research focuses on the synthesis of high performance thermoplastic polymer.

Penghua Yan received his PhD degree in college of chemistry and chemical engineering from Northwest Normal University in 2011. He is the senior engineer in Petrochemical Research Institute of China National Petroleum Corporation. His main research interests are synthesis of functional polymer materials and preparation of nano-composite materials.

Shuai Zhang obtained PhD degree in materials science from Sichuan University under the co-supervision of Prof. Yi Gu and Prof. Qiang Fu. He is now working as a post-doctor under the supervision of Prof. Xiaobo Liu at UESTC. His current research interests lie in the field of high-performance polymers and related functional materials.

Xiaobo Liu received his PhD degree in college of polymer science and engineering from Sichuan University in 1995. He became the full professor of UESTC in 2005 and is currently the director of Sichuan Province Engineering Technology Research Center of Novel CN Polymeric Materials. His main research interests are synthesis and properties of high performance thermoplastic polymer and their high performance nanocomposites.
